did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16), a deep learning model, for automated skin cancer classification (binary classification of benign vs. malignant skin lesions) using dermoscopic images from datasets like ISIC and HAM10000.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2025 5th International Conference on Intelligent Cybernetics Technology & Applications (ICICyTA). It details a specific methodology involving the Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) model trained on combined datasets (ISIC 2024, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, PROVe-AI) with three data balancing strategies (Raw, Balanced, Undersample) and two learning rates. The paper reports new quantitative results, including validation accuracy (99.92%), F1 scores, and computational efficiency metrics (GPU memory, training time), which constitutes original technical research rather than a literature review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using Vision Transformers and Data Balancing Techniques
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Intelligent Cybernetics Technology & Applications (ICICyTA)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of the Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) for binary skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images from combined datasets including ISIC 2024, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and PROVe-AI. The researchers compared three data balancing strategies—Raw, Balanced (via augmentation), and Undersampled—trained with two learning rates (0.001 and 0.0001) over 30 epochs. The Balanced dataset with a learning rate of 0.0001 achieved the highest validation accuracy of 99.92% and an F1 score of 0.9992, demonstrating stable convergence without overfitting. While the Raw dataset yielded similar accuracy (99.91%), it required significantly more GPU memory and training time, whereas the Undersampled dataset offered the fastest computation but lower accuracy (98.91%). The results indicate that balancing data with a low learning rate provides an optimal balance between accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency for automated skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes in the skin cancer classification task. The dataset contained 400,513 benign images in the RAW and Balanced scenarios, and 14,829 in the Undersample scenario.
One of the two binary classes in the skin cancer classification task. The dataset contained 14,829 malignant images in the RAW and Undersample scenarios, and 399,821 in the Balanced scenario.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Benign vs. Malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete classes: 'Benign' (class 0) and 'Malignant' (class 1). The task is explicitly defined as 'binary skin cancer classification' and 'automated skin lesion classification' in the abstract and introduction. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Combined Dataset (ISIC 2024, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, PROVe-AI)
datasets_used.description: A combined collection of dermoscopic images of benign and malignant skin lesions sourced from ISIC 2024, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and PROVe-AI. The study utilized three variations of this combined data: RAW (original distribution), Balanced (via augmentation-based oversampling), and Undersampled.

use.training: Used for training the Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) model under three balancing strategies (RAW, Balanced, Undersample) and two learning rates (0.001, 0.0001) for 30 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate performance metrics including accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall.
use.other: The RAW dataset served as a baseline for pure performance without balancing intervention. The Balanced and Undersampled datasets were used to evaluate the impact of data balancing strategies on model accuracy and computational efficiency.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study used comparative analysis of performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, computational efficiency) across different experimental conditions.

RAW Dataset.description: Original unmodified distribution (400,513 Benign, 14,829 Malignant images)
RAW Dataset.notes: The RAW dataset achieved high performance but required the most computational resources. The LR 0.001 setting showed convergence instability at the last epoch compared to LR 0.0001.

Balanced Dataset.description: Augmentation-based oversampling to equalize classes (400,513 Benign, 399,821 Malignant images)
Balanced Dataset.notes: The Balanced dataset with LR 0.0001 achieved the highest overall performance and stability. It offered a significant efficiency improvement (~19.3% time saving) compared to the RAW dataset while maintaining superior accuracy.

Undersample Dataset.description: Undersampling of majority class (14,829 Benign, 14,829 Malignant images)
Undersample Dataset.notes: The Undersample dataset yielded the fastest computation and lowest memory usage but resulted in lower accuracy compared to RAW and Balanced datasets due to loss of critical information from the majority class.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; paper notes ViT is data-intensive and often requires pre-training on large datasets, but does not specify if this specific model was pre-trained or trained from scratch.

preprocessing_techniques: Data balancing strategies: RAW (original unmodified distribution), Balanced (augmentation-based oversampling of the minority/malignant class to equalize class counts), and Undersampled (reduction of the majority/benign class to match the malignant class count)
Patch extraction with a patch size of 16x16 pixels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used in the 'Balanced' dataset strategy to equalize the number of benign and malignant samples. The malignant class (minority) was oversampled to match the benign class (majority) count, resulting in 400,513 benign images and 399,821 malignant images.

Used in the 'Undersample' dataset strategy by reducing the majority class (benign) samples to match the minority class (malignant) count. This resulted in 14,829 images for both the benign and malignant classes.

conclusions: The Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) serves as a robust and efficient backbone for automated binary skin cancer classification (benign vs. malignant) using dermoscopic images.
Data balancing strategies significantly impact model performance, stability, and computational efficiency.

The Balanced dataset (via augmentation-based oversampling) combined with a low learning rate (0.0001) achieved the optimal balance, yielding the highest validation accuracy (99.92%) and F1 score (0.9992) with stable convergence and no signs of overfitting.

The Balanced dataset configuration also offered superior computational efficiency compared to the RAW dataset, requiring approximately 19.3% less training time per epoch while maintaining high accuracy.
The RAW dataset achieved high accuracy (99.91%) but was computationally expensive, requiring 31% more GPU memory and 20% longer training time than the Balanced dataset.

The Undersampled dataset provided the fastest computation (1.70 seconds/epoch) and lowest memory usage but resulted in lower accuracy (98.91%) due to the loss of critical information from the majority class.
A low learning rate (0.0001) consistently provided more stable convergence and better performance than a high learning rate (0.001) across all dataset scenarios.
High learning rates (0.001) led to convergence instability in the RAW dataset and potential underfitting in the Balanced dataset.

Undersampling reduces dataset complexity and increases model bias, making it less ideal for clinical skin cancer detection systems where capturing all visual details is crucial, despite its utility for rapid prototyping or low-power devices.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; it relies on a combined dataset of ISIC 2024, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and PROVe-AI without reporting performance on a held-out, unseen external cohort, limiting generalizability claims.
No CNN models were tested for direct comparison; the authors note that while ViT is compared narratively to CNNs in the introduction, no additional CNN experiments were conducted in this study.

The 'Balanced' dataset was created via augmentation-based oversampling of the malignant class, which may introduce synthetic data bias or overfitting risks not fully mitigated by the reported metrics.

The 'Undersampled' dataset resulted in lower accuracy (98.91%) due to the loss of critical information from the majority benign class, highlighting a significant trade-off between computational efficiency and diagnostic sensitivity.
The paper does not report cross-dataset generalization results; future work is explicitly identified as necessary to test cross-dataset generalization and real-world applicability.

Explainable AI (XAI) techniques were not implemented in this study; the authors acknowledge this as a limitation and plan to integrate XAI (e.g., attention rollout, GradCAM) in future work to improve interpretability.

The RAW dataset showed signs of plateauing and required significantly more computational resources (31% more GPU memory and 20% longer training time) compared to the Balanced dataset, despite achieving similar accuracy (99.91% vs 99.92%).

The study uses a binary classification task (Benign vs. Malignant), which simplifies the clinical reality of skin cancer diagnosis where multiple subtypes exist; this may limit clinical relevance for detailed differential diagnosis.
